title: Dynamic Property Builder
description:
Write a function called `buildObject` that takes an array of `[key, value]` pairs and returns a new object constructed from them.

Please note:
- You must use computed property name syntax (`{ [key]: value }`) inside a loop or the `reduce` method.
- Do not use the built-in `Object.fromEntries` method.

Examples:
- `buildObject([['name', 'Alice'], ['age', 30]])` returns `{ name: 'Alice', age: 30 }`
- `buildObject([])` returns `{}`
tests: if(typeof buildObject!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'buildObject' is not defined.");const tc=[{input:[['name','Alice'],['age',30]],expected:{name:"Alice",age:30},desc:"Basic pairs"},{input:[],expected:{},desc:"Empty array"}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const res=buildObject(t.input);if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify(t.expected))throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] ${t.desc}.`)}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});if(buildObject.toString().includes('fromEntries'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Object.fromEntries used.");

title: Array Pipeline
description:
Write a function called `processNumbers` that takes an array of numbers and returns a new array after applying specific transformations.

Your task is to:
1. Filter out all negative numbers.
2. Multiply each remaining number by `2`.
3. Keep only the numbers that are strictly greater than `5`.

Please note: you must chain the `filter` and `map` methods. Do not use standard loops like `for` or `while`.

Examples:
- `processNumbers([1, -3, 4, 6, -1, 3])` returns `[8, 12]`
- `processNumbers([-1, -2])` returns `[]`
- `processNumbers([3, 4])` returns `[8]`
tests: if(typeof processNumbers!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'processNumbers' is not defined.");const tc=[{input:[1,-3,4,6,-1,3],expected:[8,12],desc:"Mixed numbers"},{input:[-1,-2],expected:[],desc:"Only negative"},{input:[3,4],expected:[8],desc:"Check boundary"}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const res=processNumbers(t.input);if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify(t.expected))throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] ${t.desc}. Expected: ${JSON.stringify(t.expected)}, got: ${JSON.stringify(res)}`)}catch(e){if(e.message.includes('Test'))throw e;throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});const fnStr=processNumbers.toString();if(/for\s*\(|while\s*\(/.test(fnStr))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Do not use loops.");if(!fnStr.includes('filter')||!fnStr.includes('map'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Must chain filter and map.");

title: Session Cart
description:
Write a function called `manageCart` that simulates a basic shopping cart using the browser's `sessionStorage`.

The function must accept two arguments:
- `action` (string): the operation to perform.
- `item` (string, optional): the item to manipulate.

Supported actions:
- `'add'`: adds the `item` to the cart array stored in `sessionStorage` under the key `'cart'`.
- `'remove'`: removes the first occurrence of the `item` from the cart.
- `'clear'`: completely empties the cart.
- `'get'`: returns the current cart as a parsed array.

The cart state must persist across function calls by properly stringifying and parsing a JSON array in `sessionStorage`.

Examples:
- `manageCart('add', 'apple')` makes the cart `['apple']`
- `manageCart('add', 'banana')` makes the cart `['apple', 'banana']`
- `manageCart('remove', 'apple')` makes the cart `['banana']`
- `manageCart('get')` returns `['banana']`
- `manageCart('clear')` makes the cart `[]`
tests: if(typeof manageCart!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'manageCart' is not defined.");const ss={store:{},getItem(k){return this.store[k]||null},setItem(k,v){this.store[k]=v},removeItem(k){delete this.store[k]},clear(){this.store={}}};const origSS=globalThis.sessionStorage;globalThis.sessionStorage=ss;try{manageCart('clear');manageCart('add','apple');manageCart('add','banana');let res=manageCart('get');if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify(['apple','banana']))throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] Incorrect cart after adds.");manageCart('remove','apple');res=manageCart('get');if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify(['banana']))throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] Incorrect cart after remove.");manageCart('clear');res=manageCart('get');if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify([]))throw new Error("[Test 3 Failed] Cart not empty after clear.")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}finally{globalThis.sessionStorage=origSS}

title: Object Inspector
description:
Write a function called `summarizeObject` that takes an object and returns a summary object.

The returned object must contain:
- `keys`: an array of all own enumerable property names.
- `values`: an array of all own enumerable property values.
- `count`: the total number of own enumerable properties.

Please note: use `Object.keys` and `Object.values`. Do not use a `for...in` loop.

Examples:
- `summarizeObject({ a: 1, b: 2 })` returns `{ keys: ['a', 'b'], values: [1, 2], count: 2 }`
- `summarizeObject({})` returns `{ keys: [], values: [], count: 0 }`
tests: if(typeof summarizeObject!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'summarizeObject' is not defined.");const tc=[{input:{a:1,b:2},exp:{keys:["a","b"],values:[1,2],count:2}},{input:{},exp:{keys:[],values:[],count:0}}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const res=summarizeObject(t.input);if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify(t.exp))throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] Expected ${JSON.stringify(t.exp)}, got ${JSON.stringify(res)}`)}catch(e){if(e.message.includes('Test'))throw e;throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});if(/for\s*\([\w\s]+\s+in\s+/.test(summarizeObject.toString()))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Used for...in.");

title: Private Counter
description:
Write a function called `createCounter` that returns an object containing three methods to manage an internal count.

The methods are:
- `increment()`: increases the internal count by `1`.
- `decrement()`: decreases the internal count by `1`.
- `getCount()`: returns the current value of the count.

Please note: the internal count variable must remain completely hidden and inaccessible from the outside. It should only be manipulated via the provided closure methods.

Examples:
- `const c = createCounter(); c.increment(); c.increment(); c.getCount()` returns `2`
- `c.decrement(); c.getCount()` returns `1`
- `c.count` returns `undefined`
tests: if(typeof createCounter!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'createCounter' is not defined.");try{const c=createCounter();if(typeof c.increment!=='function'||typeof c.decrement!=='function'||typeof c.getCount!=='function')throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] Missing methods.");c.increment();c.increment();if(c.getCount()!==2)throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] Expected 2 after 2 increments.");c.decrement();if(c.getCount()!==1)throw new Error("[Test 3 Failed] Expected 1 after decrement.");if(c.count!==undefined)throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Internal count exposed.")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}

title: Object Merger
description:
Write a function called `mergeObjects` that takes two objects and returns a new object representing a shallow merge of both.

Please note:
- Properties from the second object must overwrite identically named properties from the first object.
- The original objects must not be mutated.
- You must use the spread operator (`...`). Do not use `Object.assign`.

Examples:
- `mergeObjects({ a: 1 }, { b: 2 })` returns `{ a: 1, b: 2 }`
- `mergeObjects({ a: 1, b: 2 }, { b: 99 })` returns `{ a: 1, b: 99 }`
tests: if(typeof mergeObjects!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'mergeObjects' is not defined.");try{const o1={a:1,b:2};const o2={b:99,c:3};const res=mergeObjects(o1,o2);if(res.a!==1||res.b!==99||res.c!==3)throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] Incorrect merge.");if(o1.b!==2||o2.b!==99)throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Mutated original objects.")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}if(mergeObjects.toString().includes('Object.assign'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Used Object.assign.");

title: Flexible Sum
description:
Write a function called `flexibleSum` that accepts any number of numeric arguments and returns their total sum.

The function must handle scenarios where it is called with zero, one, or multiple arguments.

Please note: you must use the rest parameter syntax (`...args`). Do not use the legacy `arguments` object.

Examples:
- `flexibleSum()` returns `0`
- `flexibleSum(5)` returns `5`
- `flexibleSum(1, 2, 3, 4)` returns `10`
- `flexibleSum(-1, 1)` returns `0`
tests: if(typeof flexibleSum!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'flexibleSum' is not defined.");const tc=[{args:[],exp:0},{args:[5],exp:5},{args:[1,2,3,4],exp:10},{args:[-1,1],exp:0}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const res=flexibleSum(...t.args);if(res!==t.exp)throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] Expected ${t.exp}, got: ${res}`)}catch(e){if(e.message.includes('Test'))throw e;throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});if(flexibleSum.toString().includes('arguments'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Used 'arguments' object.");

title: Hoisting Detective
description:
Write a function called `getHoistedValue` that demonstrates variable hoisting in JavaScript.

Your function must:
1. Declare a variable using `var x` and assign it the value `42` somewhere within the function body.
2. Return the value of `x` before the line where the assignment occurs.

Examples:
- `getHoistedValue()` returns `undefined` because the declaration is hoisted but the assignment is not.
tests: if(typeof getHoistedValue!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'getHoistedValue' is not defined.");try{const res=getHoistedValue();if(res!==undefined)throw new Error(`[Test 1 Failed] Expected undefined due to hoisting, got ${res}`)}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}if(!getHoistedValue.toString().includes('var x'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Must use 'var x'.");

title: Equality Trap
description:
Write a function called `classifyEquality` that evaluates two arguments, `a` and `b`, and returns a string based on their equality.

The function must return:
- `'strict'` if `a === b`
- `'loose'` if `a == b` but `a !== b`
- `'none'` if neither equality condition holds.

This task tests your understanding of strict equality, type coercion, and specific edge cases like `NaN`.

Examples:
- `classifyEquality(1, 1)` returns `'strict'`
- `classifyEquality(1, '1')` returns `'loose'`
- `classifyEquality(null, undefined)` returns `'loose'`
- `classifyEquality(NaN, NaN)` returns `'none'`
tests: if(typeof classifyEquality!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'classifyEquality' is not defined.");const tc=[{a:1,b:1,exp:"strict"},{a:1,b:"1",exp:"loose"},{a:null,b:undefined,exp:"loose"},{a:NaN,b:NaN,exp:"none"},{a:0,b:false,exp:"loose"},{a:{},b:{},exp:"none"}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const res=classifyEquality(t.a,t.b);if(res!==t.exp)throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] For a=${t.a}, b=${t.b}. Expected: ${t.exp}, got: ${res}`)}catch(e){if(e.message.includes('Test'))throw e;throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});

title: Function Factory
description:
Write a function called `makeMultiplier` that takes a number `factor` as an argument and returns a new function.

The returned function must accept a single number `value` and return the product of `value` and `factor`.

Examples:
- `makeMultiplier(3)(5)` returns `15`
- `makeMultiplier(2)(0)` returns `0`
- `makeMultiplier(-1)(7)` returns `-7`
tests: if(typeof makeMultiplier!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'makeMultiplier' is not defined.");try{const mul3=makeMultiplier(3);if(typeof mul3!=='function')throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] Must return a function.");if(mul3(5)!==15)throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] 3 * 5 should be 15.");const mul2=makeMultiplier(2);if(mul2(0)!==0)throw new Error("[Test 3 Failed] 2 * 0 should be 0.");if(makeMultiplier(-1)(7)!==-7)throw new Error("[Test 4 Failed] -1 * 7 should be -7.")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}

title: Custom Sorter
description:
Write a function called `sortByField` that takes an array of objects and a string `field`, and returns a new array sorted by that specific field in ascending order.

Please note:
- Use `Array.prototype.sort` with a custom comparator.
- Do not mutate the original array; ensure you return a new one.
- The field values will always be numbers.

Examples:
- `sortByField([{n: 3}, {n: 1}, {n: 2}], 'n')` returns `[{n: 1}, {n: 2}, {n: 3}]`
- `sortByField([{age: 30}, {age: 20}], 'age')` returns `[{age: 20}, {age: 30}]`
tests: if(typeof sortByField!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'sortByField' is not defined.");const tc=[{arr:[{n:3},{n:1},{n:2}],field:'n',expected:[{n:1},{n:2},{n:3}],desc:"Sort by n"},{arr:[{age:30},{age:20}],field:'age',expected:[{age:20},{age:30}],desc:"Sort by age"}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const orig=JSON.stringify(t.arr);const res=sortByField(t.arr,t.field);if(JSON.stringify(res)!==JSON.stringify(t.expected))throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] ${t.desc}. Expected: ${JSON.stringify(t.expected)}, got: ${JSON.stringify(res)}`);if(JSON.stringify(t.arr)!==orig)throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] Original array mutated.`)}catch(e){if(e.message.includes('Test'))throw e;throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});

title: Context Binder
description:
Write a function called `bindContext` that takes an object `ctx` and a function `fn`, and returns a new function that always calls `fn` with its `this` context set to `ctx`.

Please note: do not use the built-in `Function.prototype.bind` method. You must implement the binding yourself using `call` or `apply`.

Examples:
- `const fn = bindContext({ x: 10 }, function() { return this.x; }); fn()` returns `10`
- The returned function must always use `ctx` as `this`, regardless of how it is invoked.
tests: if(typeof bindContext!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'bindContext' is not defined.");try{const obj={x:42};const fn=function(){return this.x;};const bound=bindContext(obj,fn);if(bound()!==42)throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] Expected bound function to return 42");const boundWithArgs=bindContext({y:10},function(a,b){return this.y+a+b;});if(boundWithArgs(5,5)!==20)throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] Expected bound function to pass arguments correctly")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}if(bindContext.toString().includes('.bind('))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Do not use Function.prototype.bind.");

title: Generic Pair
description:
Write two functions to manage key-value pairs.

First, write a function called `createPair` that takes two arguments of any type and returns an object with `first` and `second` properties holding those respective values.

Second, write a function called `swapPair` that takes the object returned by `createPair` and returns a brand new pair with the `first` and `second` values swapped.

Please note: do not mutate the original pair object.

Examples:
- `createPair(1, 'hello')` returns `{ first: 1, second: 'hello' }`
- `swapPair({ first: 1, second: 'hello' })` returns `{ first: 'hello', second: 1 }`
- `swapPair({ first: true, second: 42 })` returns `{ first: 42, second: true }`
tests: if(typeof createPair!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'createPair' is not defined.");if(typeof swapPair!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'swapPair' is not defined.");try{const p1=createPair(1,'hello');if(p1.first!==1||p1.second!=='hello')throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] createPair failed.");const p2=swapPair(p1);if(p2.first!=='hello'||p2.second!==1)throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] swapPair failed.");if(p1.first!==1)throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Original pair mutated.")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}

title: DOM Class Toggle
description:
Write a function called `toggleClass` that takes a DOM element (`element`) and a string (`className`) as arguments.

The function must:
- Add the `className` to the element if it is not currently present.
- Remove the `className` from the element if it is already present.

Please note: do not use the built-in `element.classList.toggle` method. Implement the logic manually using `classList.add` and `classList.remove`.

Examples:
- If `el` has the class `'active'`, calling `toggleClass(el, 'active')` will remove it.
- If `el` does not have the class `'active'`, calling `toggleClass(el, 'active')` will add it.
tests: if(typeof toggleClass!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'toggleClass' is not defined.");try{const el={classes:new Set(),classList:{add:(c)=>el.classes.add(c),remove:(c)=>el.classes.delete(c),contains:(c)=>el.classes.has(c)}};Object.defineProperty(el.classList,'toggle',{get:()=>()=>{throw new Error("toggle used")}});toggleClass(el,'active');if(!el.classes.has('active'))throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] Class not added.");toggleClass(el,'active');if(el.classes.has('active'))throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] Class not removed.")}catch(e){if(e.message==='toggle used')throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] classList.toggle used.");throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}

title: Debounce Implementation
description:
Write a function called `debounce` that takes a function `fn` and a number `delay` (in milliseconds), and returns a debounced version of `fn`.

The debounced function must:
- Delay the execution of `fn` until `delay` milliseconds have passed since the last time the debounced function was called.
- Reset the internal timer on every new call.
- Pass all provided arguments through to `fn`.

Examples:
- If called 3 times rapidly within the delay period, `fn` must be invoked only once — exactly `delay` ms after the final call.
tests: if(typeof debounce!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'debounce' is not defined.");const origSetTimeout=globalThis.setTimeout;const origClearTimeout=globalThis.clearTimeout;let timerCount=0;let clearedCount=0;globalThis.setTimeout=(cb,d)=>{timerCount++;cb();return 1;};globalThis.clearTimeout=(id)=>{clearedCount++;};try{let callCount=0;const fn=debounce((val)=>{callCount++;return val;},100);fn(1);fn(2);if(timerCount===0)throw new Error("[Test Failed] setTimeout was not called.")}finally{globalThis.setTimeout=origSetTimeout;globalThis.clearTimeout=origClearTimeout;}

title: Scope Chain Challenge
description:
Write a function called `outerScope` that demonstrates lexical scoping and closures.

Your function must:
1. Declare a variable named `count` initialized to `0` inside its body.
2. Define an inner function that increments `count` by `1` and returns the updated `count`.
3. Return that inner function.

Each time the returned function is called, it should increase and return the shared `count` variable.

Examples:
- `const fn = outerScope(); fn(); fn(); fn()` returns `3` on the final call.
- `outerScope()()` always returns `1` because it creates a fresh closure each time.
tests: if(typeof outerScope!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'outerScope' is not defined.");try{const fn=outerScope();if(typeof fn!=='function')throw new Error("[Test 1 Failed] outerScope must return a function.");if(fn()!==1)throw new Error("[Test 2 Failed] First call must return 1.");if(fn()!==2)throw new Error("[Test 3 Failed] Second call must return 2.");if(outerScope()()!==1)throw new Error("[Test 4 Failed] Fresh closure must return 1.")}catch(e){throw new Error(`[Test Error] ${e.message}`)}if(!outerScope.toString().includes('count'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Missing 'count' variable declaration.");

title: Type-Safe Formatter
description:
Write a function called `formatValue` that accepts a single value of type `string | number | boolean | null` and returns a specifically formatted string based on its exact type.

Formatting rules:
- `string`: return the value wrapped in explicit double quotes (e.g., `''hello''`).
- `number`: return the string `'Number: '` followed by the value (e.g., `'Number: 42'`).
- `boolean`: return `'Yes'` for `true` and `'No'` for `false`.
- `null`: return the string `'Empty'`.

Please note: use type narrowing techniques (like `typeof` checks and strict equality). Do not rely on generic methods like `.toString()`.

Examples:
- `formatValue('hello')` returns `''hello''`
- `formatValue(42)` returns `'Number: 42'`
- `formatValue(true)` returns `'Yes'`
- `formatValue(null)` returns `'Empty'`
tests: if(typeof formatValue!=='function')throw new Error("ReferenceError: Function 'formatValue' is not defined.");const tc=[{val:"hello",exp:'"hello"'},{val:42,exp:'Number: 42'},{val:true,exp:'Yes'},{val:false,exp:'No'},{val:null,exp:'Empty'}];tc.forEach((t,i)=>{try{const res=formatValue(t.val);if(res!==t.exp)throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Failed] For ${t.val}, expected '${t.exp}', got '${res}'`)}catch(e){if(e.message.includes('Test'))throw e;throw new Error(`[Test ${i+1} Error] ${e.message}`)}});if(formatValue.toString().includes('.toString()'))throw new Error("[Constraint Failed] Used .toString().");
